sections.title: What this section covers
sections.content:
In English we often need **it** as a **subject**, even when it does not refer to anything (no person or thing). Grammar books sometimes call this a **dummy / empty it**.

This section looks at:

• **Weather and temperature** – *It's raining. It's cold.*
• **Time and days** – *It's three o'clock. It's Monday.*
• **Distance** – *It's five kilometres to the town.*
• **It + adjective + to‑infinitive** – *It's important to read the question.*
• **It + adjective / noun + that‑clause** – *It's clear that he's tired. It's a good idea that you came early.*
• **It + seem / appear / look** – *It seems that they're late.*

sections.title: It for weather, time and distance
sections.content:
In English we **must use a subject**. With weather, time and distance we normally use **it**.

**Weather**
• It's raining.
• It's snowing.
• It's cold / It's very hot today.

**Time and days**
• It's three o'clock.
• It's late.
• It's Monday today.

**Distance**
• It's five kilometres to the town.
• It's ten minutes to the station.

In many other languages (including Czech) you can say just "Raining" or "Is three o'clock". In English you normally need **It + be**.

sections.title: It + adjective + to‑infinitive
sections.content:
We often move the real subject to the end of the sentence and use **it** at the beginning. This makes the sentence more natural and easier to start.

Pattern: **It + be + adjective + to‑infinitive**

• It's important to read the instructions.
• It's hard to wake up early.
• It's easy to get lost in this city.
• It's not necessary to answer all the questions.

Compare:
• To read the instructions is important. (possible, but not common)
• **It's important to read the instructions.** (natural)

sections.title: It + adjective / noun + that‑clause
sections.content:
We can also use **it** when the real subject is a **that‑clause** (a clause starting with *that*):

Pattern: **It + be + adjective / noun + that‑clause**

• It's clear that he is tired.
• It's obvious that they don't agree.
• It's a pity that she can't come.
• It's a good idea that you called before the exam.

Again, English prefers **It + be** at the start instead of beginning with the big clause.

sections.title: It with seem / appear / look
sections.content:
We also use **it** with verbs like **seem, appear, look** when the real idea comes later:

• It seems that they're late.
• It appears that there is a problem.
• It looks as if it's going to rain.

In conversation we sometimes drop *that*: *It seems they're late.*

sections.title: Quick summary
sections.content:
• English usually needs a **subject** – we cannot leave the subject empty.
• For **weather, time and distance**, we normally use **It + be**: It's raining. It's three o'clock. It's ten kilometres to Prague.
• For long subjects (to‑infinitive or that‑clause), we often use **It** at the start: It's important to practise. It's clear that he is tired.
• Remember: sentences like *Is raining* or *Important is to read the text* are wrong in English – we need **It**.

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: In English we need a subject. For weather we use it: It's raining.

questions.question: Which is correct?
questions.explanation: For time we normally say It's three o'clock.

questions.question: Complete the sentence: ____ important to read the instructions.
questions.explanation: Pattern: It is + adjective + to‑infinitive → It's important to read the instructions.

questions.question: Which sentence uses it correctly?

options.a: Clear is that he is tired.
options.b: That he is tired is clear.
options.c: It's clear that he is tired.
options.d: Is clear that he is tired.

questions.explanation: Natural English: It's clear that he is tired. (b is possible but formal; d is wrong – missing subject.)

questions.question: Which is most natural in English?

options.a: To get up early is difficult.
options.b: Difficult is to get up early.
options.c: It's difficult to get up early.
options.d: Is difficult to get up early.

questions.explanation: We usually start with It: It's difficult to get up early.

questions.question: Which is correct for weather?
questions.explanation: We need a subject: It's snowing.

questions.question: Choose the best sentence.
questions.explanation: For temperature we use It + be: It's very cold today.

questions.question: Which is correct for distance?

options.a: Is ten kilometres to the village.
options.b: It's ten kilometres to the village.
options.c: There is ten kilometres to the village.
options.d: Ten kilometres is to the village.

questions.explanation: We say: It's ten kilometres to the village.

questions.question: Complete: ____ hard to understand this text.
questions.explanation: Pattern: It is + adjective + to‑infinitive / clause.

questions.question: Which is correct?

options.a: Is obvious that they are angry.
options.b: It's obvious that they are angry.
options.c: Obvious is that they are angry.
options.d: That they are angry it's obvious.

questions.explanation: Use It + be + adjective + that‑clause: It's obvious that they are angry.

questions.question: Which sentence is best?

options.c: It seems that they are late.
options.d: Is seems that they are late.

questions.explanation: We say: It seems that they are late. (We need the subject it.)

error_correction_1.title: Practice: Correct the sentence (It as subject)

questions.question: Wrong: 'Is raining.' Correct the sentence.
answers: It's raining.
It is raining.
It's raining
It is raining
questions.explanation: For weather we use It + be: It's raining.

questions.question: Wrong: 'Is very cold today.' Correct the sentence.
answers: It's very cold today.
It is very cold today.
It's very cold today
It is very cold today
questions.explanation: Temperature: It's very cold today.

questions.question: Wrong: 'Is three o'clock.' Correct the sentence.
answers: It's three o'clock.
It is three o'clock.
It's three o'clock
It is three o'clock
questions.explanation: For time we say It's three o'clock.

questions.question: Wrong: 'Is five kilometres to the town.' Correct the sentence.
answers: It's five kilometres to the town.
It is five kilometres to the town.
It's five kilometers to the town.
It is five kilometers to the town.
It's five kilometres to the town
It is five kilometres to the town
It's five kilometers to the town
It is five kilometers to the town
questions.explanation: Distance: It's five kilometres to the town.

questions.question: Wrong: 'Important is to read the instructions.' Correct the sentence.
answers: It's important to read the instructions.
It is important to read the instructions.
It's important to read the instructions
It is important to read the instructions
questions.explanation: Use It + be + adjective + to‑infinitive: It's important to read the instructions.

questions.question: Wrong: 'Difficult is to wake up early.' Correct the sentence.
answers: It's difficult to wake up early.
It is difficult to wake up early.
It's difficult to wake up early
It is difficult to wake up early
questions.explanation: Natural English: It's difficult to wake up early.

questions.question: Wrong: 'Is obvious that he is angry.' Correct the sentence.
answers: It's obvious that he is angry.
It is obvious that he is angry.
It's obvious that he's angry.
It is obvious that he's angry.
It's obvious that he is angry
It is obvious that he is angry
It's obvious that he's angry
It is obvious that he's angry
questions.explanation: Use It + be + adjective + that‑clause.

questions.question: Wrong: 'Is clear that they don't agree.' Correct the sentence.
answers: It's clear that they don't agree.
It is clear that they don't agree.
It's clear that they do not agree.
It is clear that they do not agree.
It's clear that they don't agree
It is clear that they don't agree
It's clear that they do not agree
It is clear that they do not agree
questions.explanation: Again, we need It as subject: It's clear that they don't agree.

questions.question: Wrong: 'Was a good idea to call.' Correct the sentence.
answers: It was a good idea to call.
It was a good idea to call
questions.explanation: Use It + be + noun phrase: It was a good idea to call.

questions.question: Wrong: 'Is not easy to learn English.' Correct the sentence.
answers: It's not easy to learn English.
It is not easy to learn English.
It's not easy to learn English
It is not easy to learn English
questions.explanation: We normally say: It's not easy to learn English.
